result:
I think I want to write about a feeling. It’s the one you get in the brief, silent pause just after you turn off the car engine in a dark garage, but before you open the door to go inside. In that pocket of stillness, you are suspended between worlds. The world of motion, of radio chatter, of destination, is gone. The world of the house—with its responsibilities, its warmth, its particular light and smell—is waiting, but for just three heartbeats, it hasn’t claimed you yet.

You are simply *there*. In the dim glow of the dashboard lights, with the faint *tink-tink* of the engine cooling. It’s a moment that belongs to no one else. No one is expecting you in that seat. No text can reach you because you haven’t checked your phone. You are a secret to the universe, a quiet exhalation between two busy breaths.

I love those interstitial moments. The pause at the top of a staircase before descending into a party. The second of absolute dark after you close your eyes but before sleep drags you under. The hush in a theater when the house lights dim and the curtain hasn’t yet risen. They are gaps in the narrative of our lives, where we aren’t actors following a script. We are just pure, unclaimed potential.

Modern life is so hostile to these gaps. We fill them with podcasts, scrolling, pinging notifications—anything to avoid the nothing. But the nothing is where we find ourselves. It’s in the nothing that you can hear the faint hum of your own being, the baseline frequency of *you*, before it gets drowned out by the noise of the day.

Sometimes I think we are not exhausted by work or socializing, but by the constant, seamless *transitioning* between them. We’ve paved over the dirt paths between our engagements with asphalt highways, and we lost the chance to see the wildflowers that grew in the in-between. That car-in-the-garage feeling is a tiny patch of dirt path. A moment of non-belonging that is deeply, wholly yours.

So maybe I’ll sit here a little longer tonight. Engine off. Door unopened. Listening to the silence, and to the faint, grateful sigh of a mind allowed, for just a moment, to be nowhere at all.
